text: Jeffrey Ames (CS205): All the lectures on zoom so you could attend online, uploaded on youtube, but I recommend attending class to pay attention. HW is very long and also challenging. Lean is one of the worst things invented by humankind. However other resources are available to help assist with HW. Ames has no midterms. Quizzes are online. HW is 60% and Quiz is 40%.

Jeffrey Ames (CS336): Ames was an excellent professor. I got injured, and he would accommodate me even though I couldn't attend class. The exams were 70% of the grade, which was hard even though the final was an open book. But I learned quite a bit. Wish he is still at Rutgers.

Jeffrey Ames (CS336): He is a great professor and was nice. He made the finals online. However, his exams were a little hard. Even if you have online resources or open notes, it does not help much. The exams are 70% (20%+20%+30%). Homework assignments are easier for me.

Jeffrey Ames (CS214): Good. Lectures were clear, but it was too easy. It feels like I couldn't learn many things expected to be learnt during the Systems Programming lectures. There are only 4 homework. Guideline is quite clear, but sometimes you need extra explanation. Grading is so generous. I know students who can't earn a perfect score, but anyway he got it.

Jeffrey Ames (CS336): Professor Ames made exams worth 70% and on exams, multiple choice would be around 5 points each question. Some questions on exams were one tiny detail from slides that were only mentioned for 5 seconds in lecture and yet they would be worth so many points. He talks extremely quietly, where sometimes you can't hear him. He's not a mean person tho.

Aaron Bernstein (CS513): One of the best professor I've ever had at Rutgers. I took cs463 this semester, and I would say if you attend every lecture and pay attention, you will be fine. There is a lot of extra credit to improve your grade, and two lowest homeworks will be dropped.

Aaron Bernstein (CS513): The best professor at the CS department at Rutgers. I took his CS463 and he explained everything very well. Just show up to class and pay attention. If the homework gets difficult go to the office hours. He also gives a ton of extra credit for you to pull your grade up if your exams don't go well. He is a saint.
Aaron Bernstein (CS344): I learned a lot in this class because of his accepting and accessible teaching style.

Aaron Bernstein (CS344): Bernstein gives extra credit in most hw & exams. Exam is non cumulative. Lectures are recorded. Each hw usually has 1 very hard question. Exams are modified versions of the hws. He will tell you which questions the exams are similar to. Very lenient on final grade, 86 is an A even though the average is around 80+. Got an A without exam 2.
Aaron Bernstein (CS344): The goat, gives so much extra credit and a generous curve. If you have the option to take him for 344, do it!!!!!!

Alexander Borgida (CS336): It's true through Borgida's teaching you can tell he thinks like an engineer but he works with students. If you ask questions and go to his office hours as needed then you will get a good grade. It's that simple. He's not wildly entertaining and his teaching may leave you with questions but all you need to do is ask. He's a good prof.

Alexander Borgida (CS336): Alexander Borgida genuinely cares about teaching, but his lectures are structurally flawed. Borgida reads slides for an hour and a half; this is not an effective way to learn. Yet he takes attendance, so you are required to subscribe to this ineffective teaching method which, at least for me, but I suspect many others, was a waste of time.

Alexander Borgida (CS336): The lord of vagueness who mastered in the art of making students confused. Get ready to work your butt off. He will read the slides, but show up for the attendance. HW are like decoding crypted message. Demands billion features in the project. Did I just say billion? yes, BILLION in capital. TA's have no mercy grading.

Alexander Borgida (CS336): His lectures are very dry and redundant. Good thing the concepts for this subject are straight forward because this professor isn't the brightest for a CS professor. I've had worse professors though.

Alexander Borgida (CS205): Professor Borgida was a very good professor for Discrete Structures, aka CS205. His greatest strengths were his care for his students, and his very thorough visual slide decks he constructed. He always did what he could to make sure we were rewarded for putting work in. His lectures were a bit hard to understand sometimes, but self-study helps.

Ana Paula Centeno (CS112): Prof. Centeno isn't a bad professor for CS112, but I recommend doing a lot of self-studying. The assignments can be heavy, so practicing with past exams, watching YouTube videos, and doing some LeetCode problems will help you understand the material better. Stay on top of the work and you'll do fine.

Ana Paula Centeno (CS111/CS112): Unless you are completely new to programming, these classes aren't difficult. While it does seem like exams are more difficult due to their midterm grade replacement policy, that same policy makes it much easier to get more points on exams. Assignments outside of exams are simple, but lengthy; 111 exams are code-heavy; 112 exams are concept-heavy.

Ana Paula Centeno (CS111): I think I know more about her love for cats than computer science. Lectures move slow, you can just skip and study the practice exams. The study material is actually useful.

Ana Paula Centeno (CS111): But professor Centeno is a really great professor, she answers all questions, encourages questions, and teaches well. As long as you ask questions and go to lectures you will be fine. Plus they have past exams as a resource to study for future exams in the course which are very helpful.

Ana Paula Centeno (CS111/CS112): I took CS111 and CS112, and it was the worst class that I ever took as a student at Rutgers. The exams and assignments were unnecessarily hard and I do not wish for anyone to take her class ever again. I did not like taking the two courses, and I do not recommend taking those two courses under any circumstance.

Ahmed Elgammal (CS334): This class is so hard. The professor is very nice, though a little hard to understand. The content is just so hard and so math-heavy. I passed, which surprises even me, but I do not recommend if you don't like math or computer graphics/vision/ML.

Ahmed Elgammal (CS334): Honestly, Elgammal is super caring and sweet, he truly wants students to succeed, understands if we struggle, and is never judgemental. Okay lecturer but gives enough materials to do well. Final and projects were not bad, midterm/quizzes are a bit harder. He is an AI researcher, so if you don't want to spend 2 months on AI, don't take 334.
Ahmed Elgammal (CS334): He made an announcement on Saturday that there would be a quiz in two days on Monday.
Ahmed Elgammal (CS334): one of the smartest professors I've seen at Rutgers. do the homework and the problems in the book then you'll get a good grade.

Ahmed Elgammal (CS534): DO NOT select this course unless you want to spend most of your whole semester time finishing this course's COUNTLESS and DIFFICULT homework/quiz. And you will not have much time to focus on other courses.

Ahmed Elgammal (CS534): Very knowledgeable and helpful professor. He is very flexible with assignment due dates and the grading was rather lenient. He is very motivating and wants to see you take on a project that you find interesting. The only drawback was the lectures were 3 hours long.

John-Austen Francisco (CS211): Professor Francisco is a mixed bag - his lectures can be boring, but he's still a knowledgeable and accessible prof. Using unstuck study and the free textbook helped me prepare for his tough exams.

John-Austen Francisco (CS211): So far the most boring professor I've ever had. Francisco is very lecture-heavy and uses a very mundane and inefficient way of note-taking, it is nearly impossible not to fall asleep while watching this guy lecture. Assignments are straightforward however his tests are tough. Just avoid taking this professor at all costs.

John-Austen Francisco (CS211): Less work and cool guy, but he is so disorganized. He did not put in the final grades into Canvas or tell us what the grade cutoffs are but he did put in grades into the transcript, I don't even know what I got on my final. These good reviews were written by him. If you like going crazy over your grades and worrying during the holidays, take him!

John-Austen Francisco (CS211): Honestly, if you chose not to attend franny's lectures, you're really missing out. Great guy, really knows what he is talking about, funny guy, easy assignments (3 v.s 5) and midterm, avg final. It kinda pains me to see franny leave, another great prof leaving this shoddy cs department, but he's off to bigger and better things. Overall 10/10 tbh

John-Austen Francisco (CS211): Terrible lecturer. The lectures were boring and hard to follow. He's really bad at explaining concepts. The assignments weren't too bad, but the exams were pretty difficult. Pretty accessible outside of class. Nice guy tho, just bad at teaching.

Uli Kremer (CS314): Sometimes his lectures can be a little hard to follow but overall, he explains everything very well. Would 100% recommend going to office hours. Once he breaks down the material in the office hours, it is very easy. The projects don't take too long and the exams are fair.

Uli Kremer (CS415): I took Compilers with Prof. Kremer in the spring. The class was very interesting and fairly useful. Register allocator project was pretty tough. The next two were very easy. Exams were fair. Lecture notes are good but have incomplete examples filled in during lectures. He uses Piazza, which is GREAT. Homework is 5% but great exam practice!

Uli Kremer (CS314): Kremer is undeniably one of the best CS professors at Rutgers. His comprehension of the material and his delivery of the information is spectacular. If you go to every class and listen, you should get at least a B+ in this course. The programing assignments are very doable. Just start early and try to understand them. STUDY FOR THE EXAMS!

Uli Kremer (CS314): Kremer is incredibly laid back. His material is really interesting, sometimes difficult, but he is very clear and helpful about it. The hws are pretty hard but he's the God of giving extensions. Exams and projects have been fair so far. Definitely worth taking. His German accent can get a bit thick but his lectures are still very enjoyable.

Uli Kremer (CS314): Does not care about the students. If you cant make it to the office hours he will not care. Wont help you out with the material whatsoever. If u do not understand anything its not his problem. He acts like a nice person in class but hes the total opposite during office hours. Ruined my GPA just because of this single class. Worst class to take him.

Srinivas Narayana (CS553): Enthusiastic about teaching and explains concepts well. Gives a lot of resources for assignments, which are hard but fair. There is a lot of reading though, and a quiz every week. The lectures are informative and recorded. He runs the class very well and gives you every chance to do well. If he's teaching a class, take it.

Srinivas Narayana (CS416/CS518): Narayana is a great professor, but his exams are pretty hard. It is hard to predict what is on the exams. It is also because OS is also a hard course. Projects were okay. But I still think he is a great professor, and he has all his notes and recordings are available online. So you can review it. He is very helpful during office hours.

Srinivas Narayana (CS352): if this man is teaching any course, TAKE IT. he's the best cs lecturer I've ever had. if u ask him anything, he'll explain until u understand and he's very patient. even though lectures aren't mandatory and he records them, you will get an A if u go and pay attention. the exams are basically pulled from the lectures and you get a cheat sheet.

Srinivas Narayana (CS352): one of the best CS professors at Rutgers. he makes the content very easy to understand and fun. lectures are recorded and posted. all projects are group/partner projects. lets you have cheat sheets for exams too.

Srinivas Narayana (CS352): Narayana is one of the best at Rutgers and 352 was such a well-structured, interesting, and relevant course. I thoroughly recommend any CS student to take any class he teaches. Attendance is not mandatory but going to and participating in the lectures helps the material stick and despite the lectures being recorded, they are way better in person.
Pedro Pajarillo (CS111): Great Prof, you learn a lot w him! He stuck stuff in everytime i asked for extra credit but those extracurricular sessions where he went hard was worth it!

Pedro Pajarillo (CS111): Prof. Pedro is the goat. Hands down the best professor I've ever had at Rutgers, no cap. Dude didn't just teach me programming, he turned me into a W programmer. By the time I walked out of his class, I felt like I could cook up code even in my sleep.
Pedro Pajarillo (CS111): Prof Pedro is the THE BEST.
Pedro Pajarillo (CS111): Mr Pajarillo was the best CAVE Manager I have ever seen.

Lars Sorensen (CS111): CS 111 is a hard class for beginners. It takes time to learn the ins and outs. Professor Sorenson is cool, though, and he is one of the few good, caring CS professors I had at Rutgers.

Lars Sorensen (CS111): He was a decent professor and explained the concepts and ideas well in the lecture. He makes a few jokes occasionally but is very savvy about time. He makes sure we leave 5 minutes before the class ends. Just make sure you don't talk during class, or else he will get extremely angry and yell at you. Even the quietest whispers at the back won't do.
Lars Sorensen (CS111): it was okayish

Lars Sorensen (CS111): The hardest part is the homework, they are way too hard for a beginner course and if you have no coding experience, good luck. Surprisingly the HW is a lot harder than the exams, the midterm and final are pretty chill imo as long as you study. Prof's Lectures are funny and useful, although I wish he would spend more time on the slides.
Lars Sorensen (CS111): Seems to really care about the field and enjoy it. Explains the topics really well and helps understand it on more than a surface level.
